BerriAI/litellm#19125.body:
### What happened?

Using LiteLLM's `/responses` endpoint with Vercel AI SDK, `result.text` returns empty string while `textStream` works fine.

**Root cause:** `text-start` and `text-delta` SSE events have different IDs, so SDK can't match them.

```
text-start:  id = "msg_7c9e304c-..."   ← LiteLLM generates this
text-delta:  id = "AEVoabq_MuW..."     ← Gemini's raw ID leaks through
error:       "text part AEVoabq_... not found"
```

**Expected:** IDs should be consistent across `text-start` → `text-delta` → `text-end` per OpenAI spec.

**Actual:** Each event type gets a different ID, breaking SDK's text accumulation.

**Workaround:** Use Chat Completions API (`client.chat()`) instead of Responses API.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Set up LiteLLM proxy with Gemini model access

2. Install AI SDK:
```bash
npm install ai@latest @ai-sdk/openai@latest
```

3. Run this code:
```typescript
import { createOpenAI } from '@ai-sdk/openai';
import { streamText } from 'ai';

const client = createOpenAI({
  baseURL: 'https://your-litellm-proxy.com',
  apiKey: 'your-key',
});

async function test() {
  const result = streamText({
    model: client.responses('gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite'),
    prompt: 'Say "Hello World"',
  });

  // This works - chunks arrive
  let streamed = '';
  for await (const chunk of result.textStream) {
    streamed += chunk;
  }
  console.log('textStream:', streamed); // "Hello" 

  // This fails - empty string
  console.log('result.text:', await result.text); // "" 
}

test();
```

4. Observe `result.text` is empty while `textStream` works


## Additional context

**This was already fixed for Chat Completions API** in PR #14965 (issue #14962). Same bug - each chunk got different ID. Fix was to cache ID at stream start and reuse for all chunks.

Just need to apply same pattern to Responses API transformation layer.


Also tested with AI SDK:
- `ai`: v6.0.35 and v5
- `@ai-sdk/openai`: v3.0.10 and v2

**Models tested (all fail with responses(), all work with chat()):**
- gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite
- gemini/gemini-2.5-flash
- gemini/gemini-2.5-pro

**References:**
- OpenAI spec requires consistent `item_id`: https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference/responses-streaming
- LiteLLM Responses API docs: https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/response_api
- AI SDK uses responses() by default since v5: https://ai-sdk.dev/providers/ai-sdk-providers/openai
- Prior fix: https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/pull/14965

### Relevant log output

```shell
[1] type: "text-start"
{
  "type": "text-start",
  "id": "msg_7c9e304c-7618-443e-9999-3c95afe3b9ff"
}

[2] type: "text-delta"
{
  "type": "text-delta",
  "id": "AEVoabq_MuW6jrEPs46BoQ0",
  "text": "Hello World"
}

[3] type: "error"
{
  "type": "error",
  "error": "text part AEVoabq_MuW6jrEPs46BoQ0 not found"
}

[4] type: "text-end"
{
  "type": "text-end",
  "id": "msg_7209f5ca-1037-407e-a9d4-ec84522dee0c"
}

[5] type: "error"
{
  "type": "error",
  "error": "text part msg_7209f5ca-1037-407e-a9d4-ec84522dee0c not found"
}
```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

Other

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

v1.80.11

BerriAI/litellm#19152.body: ### What happened? ## Issue Currently, LiteLLM only supports 'low' and 'high' for the `media_resolution` (via the `detail` parameter) when processing images with Gemini models. However, Gemini 3 (and the Vertex AI Gemini API) officially support a `"medium"` (and a couple more) option for `media_resolution`. This makes it impossible to fully leverage the model's capabilities and control token allocation granularity. ## Current code ## Proposal Either accept the rest of the modes or add a workaround to pass a custom param to the gemini call. ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Make a `litellm.completion` call with medium detail (a message like the following) for model `vertex_ai/gemini-3-flash-preview` ```python messages = [ { "role": "user", "content": [ { "type": "image_url", "image_url": { "url": "https://example.com/photo.png", "detail": "medium" # should be mapped to media_resolution: "medium" } } ] } ] ``` You should observe in the usage stats that the image tokens used is the same as if detail is "high". ### Relevant log output ```shell ``` ### What part of LiteLLM is this about? SDK (litellm Python package) ### What LiteLLM version are you on ? 1.80.16 ### Twitter / LinkedIn details _No response_
BerriAI/litellm#19152.title: [Bug]: Add support for "medium" detail level on gemini models

### What happened?

The function of this parameter has been changed.
After testing, I found that versions >=1.80.0 basically all have this issue.
The version 1.79.3 does not have this issue.

stream_timeout → maximum time to wait for the first chunk (i.e., first token) in a streaming response.
Use this to abort “hanging” providers (e.g., Bedrock slow start) and retry another model.

https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/proxy/timeout

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Set a short time for stream_timeout param， example 2s
2. Let the model generate for a long time, such as generating for 5s.

BerriAI/litellm#19254.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

According to the documentation, you should be able to set `use_litellm_proxy` per request in `litellm.completion()` call.
https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/providers/litellm_proxy#option-3-set-per-request

However, under the `completion()` definition, when calling `get_llm_provider()`, no kwargs/litellm_params are passed in. Importantly, in `get_llm_provider()`, there is a check for `litellm.LiteLLMProxyChatConfig._should_use_litellm_proxy_by_default()` but since no kwargs are passed from `completion()` to `get_llm_provider()`, they aren't passed to `_should_use_litellm_proxy_by_default()` either. 

This function checks whether the global `use_litellm_proxy` flag is set, whether the kwarg is set, and whether the environment variable is set. If we want to use option 3 for 1 specific call, we can't do that right now and the check fails.



### Steps to Reproduce

1. Set LITELLM_PROXY_API_BASE and LITELLM_PROXY_API_KEY environment variables.
2. Test:
litellm.use_litellm_proxy = False # Make sure it is globally off
litellm.completion(
     model="vertex_ai/gemini-2.0-flash-001",
     messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello, how are you?"}],
    use_litellm_proxy=True # Set it on individual request
 )

3. You should be authenticated by the environment variables. However, this fails and you will need vertex_ai credentials, which might not exist.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

SDK (litellm Python package)

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

v1.80.16

### Twitter / LinkedIn details

_No response_
BerriAI/litellm#19254.title: [Bug]: litellm_params ignored by get_llm_provider function in completion() definition

BerriAI/litellm#19393.body: ### Check for existing issues - [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate. ### What happened? We are having issues where LiteLLM's `/metrics` endpoint starts to take ~40 seconds and return ~30MB of data due to use of the /responses api.Since the litellm_proxy_total_requests_metric metric by default includes the route label, each time a request is made to a unique path an additional metric line is added. This is usually fine except our team has started doing more batch workflows which make use of the /responses api which when called includes a parameter as a url path segment such as /responses/1234567890 (This is using the [background mode](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/background#polling-background-responses) of the Responses API. For example Datadog [registers](https://cdn.zappy.app/2420b392a85affd1ad38cb1ee0f7cb2e.png) this as /v1/responses/{response_id} They involve GET requests that include the ID in the path.). This leads to a massively growing /metrics payload which eventually starts throwing service health alerts in our monitoring. I would suggest the default be updated to either not include route in the labels to reduce cardinality or make a special case for the /responses/xxxx route to collapse them all into a single entry. It is very unlikely that we would want metrics for every different id of a response, if we needed to see that sort of information we would use our logs. ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Start the LiteLLM proxy with Prometheus enable. 2. Make multiple GET requests to /v1/responses/{response_id} with different IDs: ``` # Make requests with different response IDs curl -X GET http://localhost:4000/v1/responses/1234567890 \ -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-1234" curl -X GET http://localhost:4000/v1/responses/9876543210 \ -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-1234" curl -X GET http://localhost:4000/v1/responses/abcdefghij \ -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-1234" ``` 3. Query the /metrics endpoint: `curl http://localhost:4000/metrics | grep litellm_proxy_total_requests_metric` 4. Observe: each unique response ID creates a separate metric line with `route="/v1/responses/1234567890", route="/v1/responses/9876543210"`, etc. ### Relevant log output ```shell ``` ### What part of LiteLLM is this about? BerriAI/litellm#19625.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### The Feature

GigaChat API supports different function calling modes, including a mode where the model is required to call a function (see GigaChat documentation:
https://developers.sber.ru/docs/ru/gigachat/guides/functions/function-calling-modes#rezhimy-obrasheniya-k-funktsiyam).

At the same time, the OpenAI API provides the **tool_choice** parameter, which allows explicit control over tool/function invocation, including the tool_choice="required" option (see OpenAI documentation:
https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/function-calling#tool-choice).

Currently, LiteLLM does not properly handle or map the **tool_choice** parameter for the GigaChat provider. As a result, it is not possible to enforce mandatory function calls when using GigaChat through LiteLLM.

This proposal suggests implementing a mapping between OpenAI-style tool_choice values (specifically tool_choice="required") and the corresponding GigaChat function calling modes, so that required function invocation semantics are preserved across providers.

### Motivation, pitch

LiteLLM is often used as an abstraction layer to build provider-agnostic applications that rely on structured outputs and tool/function calling. In such applications, enforcing a mandatory function call is critical for correctness, for example when the model is expected to always return a structured response that can be safely parsed and executed.

When using OpenAI models, this behavior can be enforced via tool_choice="required". However, when switching to GigaChat through LiteLLM, the same request silently degrades: the model may return free-form text instead of calling a function, because the tool_choice parameter is not mapped to GigaChat’s function calling modes.

This inconsistency makes it difficult to use GigaChat as a drop-in replacement for OpenAI models in workflows that depend on strict tool invocation semantics. Developers must either introduce provider-specific logic or avoid GigaChat entirely for such use cases.

By supporting a proper mapping between OpenAI-style tool_choice values and GigaChat’s native function calling modes, LiteLLM can preserve consistent behavior across providers and improve portability of existing applications without requiring changes in application-level code.

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

Proxy

### LiteLLM is hiring a founding backend engineer, are you interested in joining us and shipping to all our users?

No

### Twitter / LinkedIn details

_No response_
BerriAI/litellm#19625.title: [Feature]: Add support for tool_choice="required" in GigaChat

BerriAI/litellm#19693.body:
### What happened?

After upgrading past v1.80.11, I started seeing this error on every chat completion request:

```
TypeError: Router.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'model_alias_map'
```

The traceback points to `route_llm_request.py` line 200. This seems related to #19422, but that fix appears incomplete.

### The Issue

PR #19441 added validation in `proxy_server.py` for startup-time Router initialization. That's great.

But there's a separate code path. It's in `route_llm_request.py`. It creates a per-request Router when hierarchical settings are detected:

```python
elif "user_config" in data:
    router_config = data.pop("user_config")
    user_router = litellm.Router(**router_config)  # No validation here
```

The `router_config` gets built from `_get_hierarchical_router_settings()`. This pulls settings from the database. If any invalid parameter is stored there (like `model_alias_map`), it gets passed straight to `Router.__init__()` without filtering.

### Question

Should the same parameter filtering logic from PR #19441 be applied here as well? Maybe in `route_llm_request.py` or earlier in `common_request_processing.py`?

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Upgrade to v1.80.15 or later
2. Have `router_settings` stored in database (via key, team, or global config)
3. Make a chat completion request
4. Observe the TypeError

### Relevant log output

```shell
TypeError: Router.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'model_alias_map'
  File "litellm/proxy/route_llm_request.py", line 200, in route_request
    user_router = litellm.Router(**router_config)
```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

Proxy

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

v1.81.x (also reproduced on v1.80.15)

### Twitter / LinkedIn details

_No response_

BerriAI/litellm#19849.body:
## Summary
When using LiteLLM proxy with Groq `qwen/qwen3-32b`, reasoning is missing in **streaming** responses when `tools` + `tool_choice: auto` are provided. Non‑streaming responses *do* include `message.reasoning`, and streaming **without** tools includes `<think>` tags.

## Environment
- LiteLLM version: 1.81.3
- Provider: Groq
- Model: `qwen/qwen3-32b`
- Proxy mode (OpenAI‑compatible)

## Steps to reproduce
1. Configure the model in LiteLLM with `reasoning_effort: "default"` in `litellm_params` (and `drop_params: true`).
2. Stream a chat completion **with tools**:

```bash
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $LITELLM_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model":"<litellm_model_id>",
    "messages":[{"role":"system","content":"You are a helpful assistant."},{"role":"user","content":"test"}],
    "stream": true,
    "tools":[{"type":"function","function":{"name":"noop","description":"noop","parameters":{"type":"object","properties":{},"additionalProperties":false}}}],
    "tool_choice":"auto"
  }' \
  http://localhost:4000/v1/chat/completions
```

## Actual
- Stream only emits content.
- No `<think>` tags and no reasoning deltas.
- `completion_tokens_details.reasoning_tokens` stays `0`.

## Expected
- Reasoning should be available during streaming (e.g., via `reasoning_content` deltas), or at least surfaced consistently with non‑streaming responses.

## Comparisons
- **Non‑streaming** with tools returns `message.reasoning` for the same model.
- **Streaming without tools** returns `<think>` tags in content.

## Possible cause
`GroqChatCompletionStreamingHandler` inherits `OpenAIChatCompletionStreamingHandler` and doesn’t map Groq `reasoning` fields into `reasoning_content` (OpenRouter has explicit mapping). If Groq streams reasoning separately when tools are enabled, LiteLLM may be dropping it.
BerriAI/litellm#19849.title: Groq Qwen3-32b: reasoning missing in streaming responses with tools/tool_choice

BerriAI/litellm#19923.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

## What is this?
- [x] Feature Request
- [x] Bug Report

## Summary
Prompt caching (`cache_control`) and thinking/reasoning parameters are not properly supported for MiniMax, GLM/ZAI, and Xiaomi models - both when using direct providers and through OpenRouter.

## Current Behavior

### 1. `cache_control` is stripped for all three providers
**Root cause**: All three providers extend `OpenAIGPTConfig`, which calls `remove_cache_control_flag_from_messages_and_tools()` in `transform_request()`:

```python
# litellm/llms/openai/chat/gpt_transformation.py:431
messages, tools = self.remove_cache_control_flag_from_messages_and_tools(
    model=model, messages=messages, tools=optional_params.get("tools", [])
)
```
**Affected files:**
- `litellm/llms/minimax/chat/transformation.py` - `MinimaxChatConfig` extends `OpenAIGPTConfig`
- `litellm/llms/zai/chat/transformation.py` - `ZAIChatConfig` extends `OpenAIGPTConfig`
- `litellm/llms/openai_like/` - Xiaomi uses generic openai_like provider

### 2. OpenRouter also strips cache_control for these models
**Root cause**: `CacheControlSupportedModels` only includes Claude and Gemini:

```python
# litellm/llms/openrouter/chat/transformation.py:25
class CacheControlSupportedModels(str, Enum):
    CLAUDE = "claude"
    GEMINI = "gemini"
    # Missing: minimax, glm, zai
```

### 3. thinking parameter not supported

| Provider | Direct | OpenRouter |
|----------|--------|------------|
| MiniMax | ❌ Not in `get_supported_openai_params()` | ❌ Dropped |
| GLM/ZAI | ✅ Dynamic (glm-4.7 only) | ❌ Only reasoning_effort added |
| Xiaomi | ❌ No dedicated provider | N/A |

### 4. Missing model metadata
- **MiniMax**: `supports_prompt_caching: true` exists but `supports_reasoning` is missing
- **GLM**: Only `glm-4.7` has `supports_reasoning: true` and cache costs
- **Xiaomi**: No `supports_prompt_caching` or `supports_reasoning` flags

## Expected Behavior
- `cache_control` should be passed through to MiniMax, GLM, and Xiaomi APIs
- `thinking` parameter should be supported for models that support reasoning
- OpenRouter should preserve `cache_control` for these providers
- Model metadata should accurately reflect provider capabilities

## Proposed Changes

### 1. MiniMax - Override cache_control stripping
```python
# litellm/llms/minimax/chat/transformation.py
class MinimaxChatConfig(OpenAIGPTConfig):
    def remove_cache_control_flag_from_messages_and_tools(self, model, messages, tools=None):
        # MiniMax supports cache_control - don't strip it
        return messages, tools
    
    def get_supported_openai_params(self, model: str) -> list:
        base_params = super().get_supported_openai_params(model=model)
        if litellm.supports_reasoning(model=model, custom_llm_provider="minimax"):
            base_params.append("thinking")
        return base_params + ["reasoning_split"]
```

### 2. GLM/ZAI - Override cache_control stripping
```python
# litellm/llms/zai/chat/transformation.py
class ZAIChatConfig(OpenAIGPTConfig):
    def remove_cache_control_flag_from_messages_and_tools(self, model, messages, tools=None):
        # GLM supports cache_control - don't strip it
        return messages, tools
```

### 3. OpenRouter - Add to CacheControlSupportedModels
```python
# litellm/llms/openrouter/chat/transformation.py
class CacheControlSupportedModels(str, Enum):
    CLAUDE = "claude"
    GEMINI = "gemini"
    MINIMAX = "minimax"  # NEW
    GLM = "glm"          # NEW
    ZAI = "z-ai"         # NEW

def get_supported_openai_params(self, model: str) -> list:
    supported_params = super().get_supported_openai_params(model=model)
    if litellm.supports_reasoning(model=model, custom_llm_provider="openrouter") \
       or litellm.supports_reasoning(model=model):
        supported_params.append("reasoning_effort")
        supported_params.append("thinking")  # ADD THIS
    return list(dict.fromkeys(supported_params))
```

### 4. Model metadata updates
```json
// model_prices_and_context_window.json
"minimax/MiniMax-M2.1": {
    "supports_prompt_caching": true,
    "supports_reasoning": true,
    ...
},
"zai/glm-4.6": {
    "supports_reasoning": true,
    "supports_prompt_caching": true,
    "cache_read_input_token_cost": ...,
    ...
}
```

## Environment
- LiteLLM version: latest main
- Models affected:
  - `minimax/MiniMax-M2.1`
  - `zai/glm-4.7`, `zai/glm-4.6`
  - `xiaomi_mimo/mimo-v2-flash`
  - `openrouter/minimax/*`
  - `openrouter/z-ai/*`

## Additional Context
Provider documentation confirming feature support:
- MiniMax: Supports explicit prompt caching with `cache_control` blocks
- GLM/Zhipu: Supports automatic caching (cache reads are discounted)
- Both support thinking/reasoning modes

### Related
- Prompt caching docs: https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/completion/prompt_caching
- MiniMax API docs: https://platform.minimax.chat/
- GLM/Zhipu API docs: https://docs.z.ai/

---
```

### Steps to Reproduce

1. 
2. 
3. 


### Relevant log output

```shell

```

Proxy

v1.81.0-stable

BerriAI/litellm#19984.body:
## Bug Report

When using Claude Code v2.1.23 through LiteLLM proxy to VertexAI, requests fail because the `anthropic-beta` header contains a value that VertexAI does not support.

### Error Message

```
API Error: 400
{"type":"error","error":{"type":"invalid_request_error","message":"Unexpected value(s) `prompt-caching-scope-2026-01-05` for the `anthropic-beta` header. Please consult our documentation at docs.anthropic.com or try again without the header."}}
```

### Environment

- **LiteLLM version**: v1.81.0-stable (latest)
- **Endpoint**: `/v1/messages?beta=true` (Anthropic passthrough)
- **Backend**: VertexAI (`vertex_ai/claude-sonnet-4-5@20250929`)
- **Client**: Claude Code v2.1.23

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure LiteLLM with VertexAI Anthropic models
2. Use Claude Code v2.1.23 pointing to LiteLLM proxy
3. Make any request - Claude Code automatically sends `anthropic-beta: prompt-caching-scope-2026-01-05`
4. Request fails with 400 error

### Root Cause

Claude Code 2.1.23 sends the `anthropic-beta: prompt-caching-scope-2026-01-05` header for prompt caching. This header is valid for the direct Anthropic API but VertexAI does not support it.

LiteLLM forwards this header without filtering unsupported values when routing to VertexAI.

### Expected Behavior

LiteLLM should filter unsupported `anthropic-beta` header values when routing requests to VertexAI, similar to how PR #18037 fixed the `structured-outputs-2025-11-13` header issue.

### Related Issues

- #17755 - VertexAI structured-outputs beta header (fixed by PR #18037)
- #14293 - Cache-enabled requests to Vertex fail due to invalid cache header
- PR #18037 - Fix: skip adding beta headers for vertex ai

### Suggested Fix

Either:
1. Add `prompt-caching-scope-2026-01-05` to the list of beta headers to filter for VertexAI
2. Implement an allowlist approach where only known-supported beta headers are forwarded to VertexAI
3. Strip all `anthropic-beta` headers for VertexAI passthrough and let VertexAI add its own supported headers

Option 2 or 3 would be more future-proof as new beta headers are added frequently.

### Workaround

Currently users must rollback to Claude Code v2.1.22 which does not send this header.
BerriAI/litellm#19984.title: [Bug]: VertexAI Anthropic passthrough fails with prompt-caching-scope-2026-01-05 beta header

BerriAI/litellm#20050.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

A bug happened!

### Steps to Reproduce

1. async def edit_image():
    response = await litellm.aimage_edit(
        model="gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-image",
            image=open("dited_image.png", "rb"),
            prompt="Change the background from water to land",
            size="1792x1024",
    )
    if response.data and len(response.data) > 0:
        image_object = response.data[0]

        # Check if we have b64_json (base64 encoded image)
        if image_object.b64_json:
            # Decode the base64 string to binary data
            image_data = base64.b64decode(image_object.b64_json)

            # Save to file
            output_path = "edited_image_1.png"
            with open(output_path, "wb") as f:
                f.write(image_data)

            print(f"Image saved to: {output_path}")
            return output_path

# Run the async function

print(response)

BerriAI/litellm#20099.body:
## Summary

The main Integrations page (https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/integrations) provides an incomplete overview of LiteLLM's integration ecosystem. The page lists only ~6 integrations while the actual codebase and documentation contain **70+ integrations** across multiple categories.

### Current 

The Integrations page currently shows:
- **AI Agent Frameworks**: Letta
- **Development Tools**: OpenWebUI
- **Observability & Monitoring**: Langfuse, Prometheus, PagerDuty

However, the sidebar and codebase reveal a much richer ecosystem that is not reflected on this landing page.

### Integration Taxonomy

Based on codebase (`/litellm/integrations/` and `/docs/`), LiteLLM integrations should be categorized as:

#### AI Tools & Frameworks
Development tools that integrate with LiteLLM Proxy:
- **IDEs**: Cursor, OpenCode
- **AI Assistants**: Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Gemini CLI, Qwen Code CLI
- **UIs**: OpenWebUI
- **Agent Frameworks**: Letta

#### Observability & Logging (28+ integrations)
Callback-based integrations for LLM request/response telemetry:
- **Analytics Platforms**: Langfuse, LangSmith, Arize, Braintrust, Opik, Weave, MLflow, PostHog
- **APM & Monitoring**: Datadog, OpenTelemetry, Prometheus.
- **Specialized**: Lunary, Athina, Galileo, Greenscale, PagerDuty, Traceloop, AgentOps.

#### Guardrails & Safety (30+ integrations)
Pre/post-call request filtering for security (Proxy-specific):
- **PII & Secret Detection**: Presidio PII Masking, Secret Detection
- **Prompt Security**: Lakera AI, Prompt Security, LLM Guard
- **Enterprise Platforms**: Bedrock Guardrails, Azure Content Safety, Aporia, Guardrails AI
- **Model Protection**: HiddenLayer, Model Armor, PANW Prisma AIRS

#### Billing & Cost Tracking
- Lago, OpenMeter

#### Prompt Management (Beta)
- LiteLLM native prompts, Custom prompt management

####. Infrastructure & Storage
- S3, GCS, Azure Storage, DynamoDB

### SDK vs Proxy Integrations

The documentation should clarify which integrations work with:
- **SDK only**: Can be used directly with `litellm.completion() or others`
- **Proxy only**: Requires LiteLLM Proxy server (e.g., guardrails, alerting)
- **Both**: Works in both contexts.

### Suggestions

1. **Restructure the landing page** to show all 6-7 categories with card-based navigation (similar to Cookbook page)
2. **Add integration counts** per category (e.g., "Observability (28+)")
3. **Clarify SDK vs Proxy compatibility** for each integration
4. **Add a search/filter** functionality for finding integrations
5. **Include logos/icons** for visual recognition (assets already exist in `/out/assets/logos/`)

### References

- Integration code: `/litellm/integrations/` 
- Observability docs: `/docs/my-website/docs/observability/` 
- Guardrails docs: `/docs/my-website/docs/proxy/guardrails/`
- Sidebar config: `/docs/my-website/sidebars.js`
- UI callback configs: `/litellm/integrations/callback_configs.json`
BerriAI/litellm#20099.title: [Docs] Restructure Integrations page with proper taxonomy and complete the list

`{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":"server-error","error":{"code":-32600,"message":"Session not found"}}`

The error is intermittent: sometimes the connection works, but after a reload/reconnect, it fails with the same error.

  > MCP Server running during tests
  > MCP Server running during VSCode connection attempts

I will attach these log files to this issue for further investigation.

VSCode should be able to connect to the MCP Server reliably, without encountering "Session not found" errors after reloads or reconnects.

### References

BerriAI/litellm#20364.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

When using Bedrock Claude models with streaming responses that contain multiple consecutive assistant messages, if any assistant message has empty content (`"content": ""`), Bedrock returns an error:


`litellm.BadRequestError: BedrockException - {"message":"The text field in the ContentBlock object at messages.X.content.Y is blank. Add text to the text field, and try again."}`

## Root Cause

Bedrock Converse API does not filter out assistant messages with empty content before sending to AWS. The direct Anthropic API path uses `anthropic_messages_pt()` which filters empty strings, but Bedrock Converse uses `AmazonConverseConfig._async_transform_request()` which bypasses this filtering entirely.

## Affected

- All Bedrock Claude models using Converse API
- Streaming responses with multiple assistant message chunks
- Version: v1.80.0 and later (confirmed)

### Steps to Reproduce

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Send a request to Bedrock Claude with multiple consecutive assistant messages where some have empty content:

```
curl -X POST "http://localhost:4000/v1/chat/completions" \
 -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
 -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-123" \
 -d '{
   "model": "bedrock/anthropic.claude-3-5-haiku-20241022-v1:0",
   "messages": [
     {"role": "user", "content": "Say hello"},
     {"role": "assistant", "content": "Hello"},
     {"role": "assistant", "content": " there"},
     {"role": "assistant", "content": "!"},
     {"role": "assistant", "content": ""},
     {"role": "assistant", "content": ""},
     {"role": "user", "content": "How are you?"}
   ]
 }'
```

2. Observe the error:

```
litellm.BadRequestError: BedrockException - {"message":"The text field in the ContentBlock object at messages.1.content.X is blank. 
Add text to the text field, and try again."}
```

## Expected Behavior

Empty assistant messages should be filtered out (or merged with non-empty content) before sending to Bedrock, similar to how the direct Anthropic API handles them.

## Actual Behavior

Empty assistant messages are sent to Bedrock, which rejects them with a validation error.

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

_No response_

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

v1.80.0

### Twitter / LinkedIn details

_No response_

### Check for existing issues

### What happened?

A bug happened!
Gemini-3 encounters errors during multi-turn tool calling. 

### Steps to Reproduce

You can use this code to reproduce the issue: 
```
import json
import litellm

API_KEY = "xxx"
API_BASE = "xxxxx"
MODEL = "gemini/gemini-3-flash-preview"

tools = [{
    'type': 'function',
    'function': {
        'name': 'get_weather',
        'parameters': {
            'type': 'object',
            'properties': {'city': {'type': 'string'}}
        }
    }
}]


def execute_tool(tool_name: str, arguments: dict) -> str:
    if tool_name == "get_weather":
        city = arguments.get("city", "unknown")
        return json.dumps({"city": city, "temperature": "15°C", "condition": "Cloudy"})
    return json.dumps({"error": "Unknown tool"})


messages = [{'role': 'user', 'content': 'What is the weather in Berlin?'}]
max_turns = 5

for turn in range(max_turns):
    print(f"\n=== Turn {turn + 1} ===")
    print(f"Messages: {json.dumps(messages, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)}")

    resp = litellm.completion(
        model=MODEL,
        messages=messages,
        tools=tools,
        api_key=API_KEY,
        api_base=API_BASE,
        temperature=0.8,
    )

    assistant_msg = resp.choices[0].message
    print(f"Response: {assistant_msg}")

    msg_dict = assistant_msg.model_dump(exclude_none=True)
    messages.append(msg_dict)

    if assistant_msg.tool_calls:
        print(f"Tool calls: {len(assistant_msg.tool_calls)}")
        for tc in assistant_msg.tool_calls:
            tool_name = tc.function.name
            tool_args = json.loads(tc.function.arguments)
            print(f"  Executing: {tool_name}({tool_args})")

            result = execute_tool(tool_name, tool_args)
            print(f"  Result: {result}")

            messages.append({
                'role': 'tool',
                'tool_call_id': [tc.id](http://tc.id/),
                'content': result,
            })
    else:
        print("Final response received.")
        break

print("\n=== Done ===")
```

litellm.exceptions.BadRequestError: litellm.BadRequestError: GeminiException BadRequestError - {"error":{"message":"* ***.contents[2].parts[0].data: required oneof field 'data' must have one initialized field\n","type":"upstream_error","param":"","code":400}}
```

BerriAI/litellm#20741.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

# Azure OpenAI Image Generation Confuses Deployment Name with Base Model Name

## Description
When invoking image generation for Azure OpenAI image models (e.g., gpt-image-1), there's a confusion between the deployment name and the base model name in the parameter handling. This causes potentially wrong model references, when deployment name is different from model name

## Current Behavior
In `/azure.py` at line 1043-1045:

The data payload is constructed with the `model` field instead of the `base_model` field

```python
            data = {"model": model, "prompt": prompt, **flattened_params}
)
```

This is propagated to the final httpx_request.
```python
            httpx_response: httpx.Response = await self.make_async_azure_httpx_request(
                client=None,
                timeout=timeout,
                api_base=img_gen_api_base,
                api_version=api_version,
                api_key=api_key,
                data=data,
                headers=headers,
            )
```

## Expected Behavior
The data["model"] key should contain the `base_model` name (e.g., "gpt-image-1") instead of the deployment name.

## Root Cause
The issue stems from the fact that:

- **Data Dict Contains  Model**: The data dictionary's "model" key contains the deployment name (like "gpt-image-15"), not the base model like "gpt-image-1.5"

## Proposed Solution
-  Ensure the data["model"] contains the base_model name for proper request formatting


## Other considerations
The error logs indicate that the /v1/responses is invoked with the model passed in the POST request, which is strange. The [API spec](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/openai/reference?view=foundry-classic#image-generation) doesn't suggest "model" should be supported at all, however [their code examples](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/openai/how-to/dall-e?view=foundry&preserve-view=true&tabs=gpt-image-1#call-the-image-generation-api) show it is..

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Azure deployment with name different from model i.e `gpt-image-15` (without '.') 
2. gpt-image-1.5 config 
```yaml
....
     base_model: gpt-image-1.5 
     model: azure/gpt-image-15 # deployment name is different 
....
```
4. Make a request to the model

### Relevant log output

```shell
Error fetching response:Error: 400 litellm.BadRequestError: litellm.ContentPolicyViolationError: {
"error": {
"message": "Model not supported with Responses API. Supported models are: ['gpt-image-1', 'gpt-image-1-mini', 'gpt-image-1.5']",
"type": "invalid_request_error",
"param": null,
"code": null
}
}
model=openai_gpt_image_15. content_policy_fallback=None. fallbacks=None.


Set 'content_policy_fallback' - https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/routing#fallbacks. Received Model Group=openai_gpt_image_15
Available Model Group Fallbacks=None
```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

Proxy

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

v1.81.9

_No response_

### Check for existing issues

### The Feature

Hi, I would like to request to have the option to choose individual models for perplexity deep research (e.g `perplexity/deep-research/opus-4.5`) to be included as acceptable arguments into `model` parameter. Additionally, I'm also requesting for Perplexity's model fallback feature to be integrated into the `model` parameter.

### Motivation, pitch

I'm working on leveraging Perplexity's feature for [model selection](https://docs.perplexity.ai/docs/agentic-research/models) and [model fallback](https://docs.perplexity.ai/docs/agentic-research/model-fallback) to be able to test and choose models that would be best suited for Deep Research for our use case. This would also help provide backup models when the primary model fails.

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

SDK (litellm Python package)

### LiteLLM is hiring a founding backend engineer, are you interested in joining us and shipping to all our users?

No

Use OpenCode or other Agent loops that need tool calling 3. BerriAI/litellm#21346.body:
## Context

OpenAI offers multiple API surfaces. For LLM completions, there are two formats: Chat Completions (`/v1/chat/completions`) and Responses API (`/v1/responses`). Different models support different endpoints — most support Chat Completions, some support both, and some newer models (GPT-5 Codex, o3-pro, o3-deep-research) only support the Responses API.

LiteLLM provides bridges between these formats:
- **Bridge A** (Responses → Chat Completions): allows `litellm.completion()` for models that only support the Responses API. Lives in `litellm/completion_extras/litellm_responses_transformation/`
- **Bridge B** (Chat Completions → Responses): allows `litellm.responses()` for models that only support Chat Completions. Lives in `litellm/responses/litellm_completion_transformation/`

Both bridges internally call the other API through the core path (`litellm.responses()` and `litellm.completion()` respectively) — the actual API call is not duplicated. But the format transformation logic encodes the same mapping knowledge twice, independently.

## The problem

Converting between these two formats requires knowing the relationship between their fields. That knowledge — the "dictionary" — is written twice, once per bridge direction. When one bridge gets a fix, the other doesn't because they don't share code.

Concrete example: the bug in #21331 (parallel tool call indices) existed only in Bridge A because it hardcoded `index=0`. Bridge B already had correct index handling via `_get_or_assign_tool_output_index()`. Same mapping, two implementations, one had the bug.

## 7 duplicated mappings found

| # | Mapping | Bridge A (Responses→CC) | Bridge B (CC→Responses) | ~Lines |
|---|---------|------------------------|------------------------|--------|
| 1 | Status ↔ finish_reason | `_map_responses_status_to_finish_reason` | `_map_completion_finish_reason_to_responses_status` | 20 |
| 2 | Provider-specific fields extraction | normalize-to-dict boilerplate × 3 | same boilerplate × 2 | 40 |
| 3 | Tool format conversion | `_convert_tools_to_responses_format` | `transform_responses_api_tools_to_chat_completion_tools` | 30 |
| 4 | Response format ↔ text format | `_transform_response_format_to_text_format` | `_transform_text_format_to_response_format` | 20 |
| 5 | Usage field names | `_transform_response_api_usage_to_chat_usage` | `_transform_chat_completion_usage_to_responses_usage` | 40 |
| 6 | Annotation ↔ citation conversion | `_convert_annotations_to_chat_format` | `_transform_citation_to_annotation` | 25 |
| 7 | Role/content block mapping | inline dict transforms | inline dict transforms | 15 |

Total: ~190 lines of duplicated domain knowledge.

The status↔finish_reason mapping already has an unintentional asymmetry that illustrates the risk:

```python
# Bridge A: Responses → Chat Completions
STATUS_TO_FINISH_REASON = {
    "completed": "stop",
    "incomplete": "length",
    "failed": "stop",       # ← lossy
    "cancelled": "stop",    # ← lossy
}

# Bridge B: Chat Completions → Responses (separate file, no cross-reference)
FINISH_REASON_TO_STATUS = {
    "stop": "completed",         # ← "failed" and "cancelled" are lost
    "tool_calls": "completed",
    "length": "incomplete",
    "content_filter": "incomplete",
}
```

Is this asymmetry intentional? Nobody can tell — the two halves live in different files with no cross-reference.

## What should NOT be unified

Each bridge has its own transformation logic — one flattens a rich format (Responses to Completions), the other constructs one (Completions to Responses). Only the field mapping "dictionary" is shared.

## Proposed refactor

Extract a shared module (e.g., `litellm/format_mapping/responses_chat_completions.py`) with:
- Bidirectional status↔finish_reason mapping with explicit asymmetry documentation
- Shared `extract_provider_specific_fields(obj) -> Optional[dict]` utility
- Paired tool format conversions
- Paired response_format↔text.format conversions
- Paired usage field mapping
- Shared constants for field name translations (`call_id` ↔ `id`, etc.)

Both bridges import from this module. ~190 lines reduced to ~80-90. Asymmetries become explicit and co-located.

## Scope

Refactor only — no user-facing behavior change.

## Related

- #21331 — Bug caused by this duplication (parallel tool call indices)

## Type

🧹 Refactoring
BerriAI/litellm#21346.title: Shared mapping utilities between Responses API and Chat Completions bridges

BerriAI/litellm#21386.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

When serving models with vllm (which is OpenAI compatible) sometimes the reasoning_parser returns the thinking chunks as "reasoning_content" in AssistantMessage but for newer models it's returning "reasoning" instead. 

For streaming calls, LiteLLM is checking for [reasoning_content only while it drops reasoning](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/main/litellm/litellm_core_utils/streaming_chunk_builder_utils.py#L460) and sets in response `reasoning_content` [here](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/main/litellm/main.py#L7297), so for new models like GLM5 all thinking chunks are dropped with streaming calls. We should be able to support both reasoning_content or reasoning. 

(Note: chunks seems to be returned fine with non-streaming calls)

### Steps to Reproduce

Serve GLM5 using VLLM

Send any completion stream request


### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

_No response_

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

1.81.1

### Twitter / LinkedIn details

_No response_

BerriAI/litellm#21446.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

I'm having issues setting up Claude Code to work with Gemini models (specifically `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash` and `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite`), although I've had success setting it up with Anthropic models served via AWS Bedrock.

An important detail is that LiteLLM seems to handle direct inference requests properly:
```plaintext
curl -X POST "http://localhost:4000/v1/messages?foo=bar" \
     -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-ant-dev-abcde12345" \
     -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
     -d '{
       "model": "gemini-2.5-flash-lite",
       "max_tokens": 100,
       "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "who played agent smith in the matrix?"}]
     }'

{"id":"1RuVaeeQK4jgz7IPpZXUuAQ","type":"message","role":"assistant","model":"gemini-2.5-flash-lite","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":9,"output_tokens":13,"total_tokens":22},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"**Hugo Weaving** played Agent Smith in The Matrix films."}],"stop_reason":"end_turn"}
```
but when it comes to do the same via Claude Code, it gets stuck having tools like `Web Search` failing and/or some other incompatibilities getting in the way.

Another relevant detail is that I'm also using a helper function in my `~/.zshrc` file:
```bash
function claude-gemini() {
    # 1. Point Claude Code to your Local LiteLLM Proxy
    export ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL="http://localhost:4000"
    export ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN="sk-ant-dev-abcde12345"

    # 2. Clear Bedrock vars to ensure LiteLLM handles the request
    unset CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK
    unset AWS_BEDROCK_SERVICE_TIER
    # export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_EXPERIMENTAL_BETAS=1

    # Force Claude Code to stay "simple"
    # export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_SEARCH=1
    # export CLAUDE_CODE_ENABLE_TASKS=false
    # export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_TASKS=1
    # export ENABLE_TOOL_SEARCH=auto:5
    # export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_BACKGROUND_TASKS=1
    # export CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC=1   # Disables non-critical networking (telemetry/update checks)

    # 3. Handle model selection (Defaults to 'auto' if no argument is passed)
    local selection="${1:-gemini-flash-2.5-lite}"
    case "$selection" in
        "flash-2.5")
            # Force Gemini 1.5 Pro (will fail if you hit the 50-request limit)
            export GEMINI_MODEL='gemini-2.5-flash'
            echo "💎 Mode: Gemini 2.5 Flash"
            ;;
        "flash-2.5-lite")
            # Force Gemini 1.5 Flash (faster, 1,500 requests/day limit)
            export GEMINI_MODEL='gemini-2.5-flash-lite'
            echo "🤖 Mode: Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite"
            ;;
    esac

    # Set the small model for background tasks (summarization, etc.)
    export ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL='gemini-2.5-flash-lite'

    echo "🚀 Starting Claude Code via LiteLLM Proxy ($GEMINI_MODEL)..."
    claude --model "$GEMINI_MODEL"   # The LiteLLM docs suggest running it this way
}
```

I've experimented with several config options both particular to LiteLLM and also for Claude Code without much success.

Some useful resources to replicate my config are:
* [litellm-config.yml](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25378820/litellm-config-cleaned.yml)
* [docker-compose.yml](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25378937/docker-compose.yml)

Some useful resources providing more details/logs from my issue are: 
* [LiteLLM logs when asking a simple question via Claude Code](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25379239/claude-code.log)

Specific versions used:
**OS**: Ubuntu 22.04
**Claude Code**: v2.1.45
**LiteLLM**: Current Version = 1.81.12

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Setup Claude Code
2. Start LiteLLM from the `docker-compose.yml` and `litellm-config.yml` files provided (didn't provide .env due to privacy concerns)
3. Add the `claude-gemini` helper function (optional)
4. Run `claude-gemini` or `claude --model gemini-2.5-flash-lite`
5. Ask something to Claude Code

BerriAI/litellm#21554.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

When setting `completion_model` in `general_settings` in `config.yaml`, I expect it to act as a **fallback default** — only used when the client request does **not** specify a model.

The [official docs (`config_settings.md`)](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/9a71ba3505422eec7906df440e955c8dc9161a94/docs/my-website/docs/proxy/config_settings.md#L206) explicitly state:

> `completion_model` — *"The default model to use for completions when `model` is not specified in the request"*

**However**, when a client sends a request with an explicit `model` in the payload, the proxy **ignores the client's model entirely** and always routes the request to whatever `completion_model` is set to in `general_settings`. The `completion_model` value acts as a hard override rather than a fallback — any model specified by the client in the request body is silently discarded.

For example, with `completion_model: "gpt-5-nano"` configured, a request that explicitly sets `"model": "claude-haiku"` will still be routed to `gpt-5-nano`:

```json
{
  "model": "claude-haiku",
  "messages": [...]
}
```

---

### Root cause

The bug is in the model resolution logic in [`common_request_processing.py` (lines 584–588)](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/9a71ba3505422eec7906df440e955c8dc9161a94/litellm/proxy/common_request_processing.py#L584-L588):

```python
self.data["model"] = (
    general_settings.get("completion_model", None)  # server default
    or user_model  # model name passed via cli args
    or model  # for azure deployments
    or self.data.get("model", None)  # default passed in http request
)
```

Because `completion_model` is evaluated **first** in the Python `or` chain, it is always truthy when set and **always wins** — the client-specified model (`self.data.get("model")`) is dead last and can never take effect.

The same bug exists in [`pass_through_endpoints.py` (lines 168–172)](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/9a71ba3505422eec7906df440e955c8dc9161a94/litellm/proxy/pass_through_endpoints/pass_through_endpoints.py#L168-L172):

```python
data["model"] = (
    general_settings.get("completion_model", None)  # server default
    or user_model  # model name passed via cli args
    or data.get("model", None)  # default passed in http request
)
```

---

### Expected behavior (per documentation)

The client-specified model should take priority. `completion_model` should only be used as a fallback when no model is provided in the request, **as documented**.

---

### Observed behavior

- The request is silently routed to the `completion_model` even though a different model was explicitly requested in the payload.
- The [`_override_openai_response_model`](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/9a71ba3505422eec7906df440e955c8dc9161a94/litellm/proxy/common_request_processing.py#L251-L265) function then **masks** this mismatch by patching the response `model` field back to the originally requested model, hiding the fact that a different model was actually called.

---

### Docs inconsistency: `completion_model` vs `image_generation_model` / `embedding_model`

The sibling settings [`image_generation_model`](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/9a71ba3505422eec7906df440e955c8dc9161a94/docs/my-website/docs/proxy/config_settings.md#L240) and [`embedding_model`](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/blob/9a71ba3505422eec7906df440e955c8dc9161a94/docs/my-website/docs/proxy/config_settings.md#L253) explicitly say *"ignores model set in request"* (i.e. hard override), while `completion_model` says *"when model is not specified in the request"* (i.e. fallback). Yet the code treats all three as hard overrides.

---

### Question: What is the intended behavior?

There seem to be two valid but distinct use cases here, and the current implementation conflates them:

1. **Fallback / default** — Use a default model only when the client omits `model` from the request. This is what the docs describe and what users would typically expect from a "default".
2. **Hard override** — Always force a specific model regardless of what the client requests. This is what the code actually does today.

Both behaviors are useful, so it may be worth considering **separate, explicit parameters** rather than overloading `completion_model` with ambiguous semantics. For example:

| Parameter | Behavior |
|---|---|
| `completion_model_default` (or `completion_model_fallback`) | Used only when the client does **not** specify a model. Acts as a fallback. |
| `completion_model_override` | Always forces this model, ignoring whatever the client sends. Analogous to how `image_generation_model` / `embedding_model` work today. |

This would make the behavior explicit and avoid the current situation where the docs say one thing and the code does another. It would also align `completion_model` behavior with the existing distinction already visible in the docs for other `*_model` settings.

At minimum, the current `completion_model` behavior should match the documentation (i.e. act as a fallback, not an override).

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Set up config.yaml as follows:

```yaml
general_settings:
  completion_model: "gpt-5-nano"
model_list:
  - model_name: "claude-haiku"
    litellm_params:
      model: anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5
  - model_name: "gpt-5-nano"
    litellm_params:
      model: openai/gpt-5-nano
```

2. Start LiteLLM Proxy.
3. Send a POST `/chat/completions` request with the following payload:

```json
{
  "model": "claude-haiku",
  "messages": [
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Hello"
    }
  ]
}
```

4. Observe that the backend actually calls `gpt-5-nano` instead of `claude-haiku`, despite `model` being explicitly set in the request.

### Relevant log output

```shell
Example log line showing the mismatch:
LiteLLM Proxy:WARNING: common_request_processing.py:304 - litellm_call_id=...: response model mismatch - requested='claude-haiku' downstream='gpt-5-nano-2025-08-07'. Overriding response.model to requested model.
```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

Proxy

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

v1.81.9

### Twitter / LinkedIn details

_No response_
BerriAI/litellm#21554.title: [Bug]: completion_model in general_settings always overrides client-specified model instead of being used as fallback

BerriAI/litellm#21702.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

The `/v1/audio/speech` endpoint does not work with `vertex_ai/` Gemini TTS models, despite being documented in the [Text-to-Speech docs](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/text_to_speech). Requests fail with `400 INVALID_ARGUMENT` from Vertex AI.

`VertexGeminiConfig.get_supported_openai_params()` in `litellm/llms/vertex_ai/gemini/vertex_and_google_ai_studio_gemini.py` (~line 275) does not include `"audio"` in its return list. The `audio` parameter is silently filtered out before reaching `map_openai_params()` (~line 659), which already has the correct `audio` → `speechConfig` mapping.

The speech-to-completion bridge correctly sends `modalities: ["audio"]` and `audio: {"voice": "..."}` to `litellm.completion()`, but by the time the request reaches Vertex AI, the `audio` param has been dropped. Vertex receives audio modality with no speech config and returns `400 INVALID_ARGUMENT`.

**Note:** This is specific to the `vertex_ai/` provider. The `gemini/` (AI Studio) provider may not be affected.

**Expected behavior:** `/v1/audio/speech` with `vertex_ai/gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts` should return audio, matching the documented curl example.

---

> Related issues: #18178 (same provider, closed with docs-only workaround in #20255), #17735 (same provider, closed by #17851 which fixed a different layer). The underlying code bug — `"audio"` missing from `VertexGeminiConfig.get_supported_openai_params()` — was not addressed in either fix.

### Steps to Reproduce

**Config:**

```yaml
model_list:
  - model_name: vertex-tts
    litellm_params:
      model: vertex_ai/gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts
      vertex_project: my-project
      vertex_location: us-central1
      vertex_credentials: /path/to/creds.json
```

**Test 1 — `/v1/audio/speech` (fails):**

```bash
curl http://0.0.0.0:4000/v1/audio/speech \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-1234" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "vertex-tts",
    "input": "Hello world, this is a test.",
    "voice": "Kore"
  }' --output speech.wav
```

Result: `500` → logs show `Vertex_aiException BadRequestError - {"error": {"code": 400, "message": "Request contains an invalid argument.", "status": "INVALID_ARGUMENT"}}`.

**Test 2 — `/v1/chat/completions` (also fails without workaround):**

```bash
curl http://0.0.0.0:4000/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-1234" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "vertex-tts",
    "modalities": ["audio"],
    "audio": {"voice": "Kore", "format": "pcm16"},
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Say hello world"}]
  }'
```

Same `400 INVALID_ARGUMENT` — the `audio` param is silently dropped.

**Test 3 — with `allowed_openai_params` workaround (succeeds):**

Adding `allowed_openai_params: [audio]` to the model config makes Test 2 return audio successfully. This confirms the mapping logic works — only the supported params list is missing `"audio"`.

Note: the `allowed_openai_params` workaround does **not** fix `/v1/audio/speech` (Test 1), because the speech-to-completion bridge calls `litellm.completion()` internally and deployment-level config overrides aren't passed through.


### Relevant log output

```shell
litellm.exceptions.BadRequestError: litellm.BadRequestError: Vertex_aiException BadRequestError - {
  "error": {
    "code": 400,
    "message": "Request contains an invalid argument.",
    "status": "INVALID_ARGUMENT"
  }
}
 LiteLLM Retried: 2 times
```

BerriAI/litellm#21891.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

When calling `/responses` on the LiteLLM proxy with `model: "glm-4.7-flash-reap"` (hosted via `hosted_vllm` and managed through Router), every request failed with HTTP 500.

The client observed errors like:

```text
LiteLLM /responses API error (500): {"error":{"message":"litellm.APIConnectionError: APIConnectionError: Hosted_vllmException - 'NoneType' object is not a mapping
No fallback model group found for original model_group=glm-4.7-flash-reap.
..."}}
``

Proxy logs and Grafana traces show that the underlying error is a `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not a mapping` thrown while handling OpenAI-compatible parameters (specifically around `extra_body`) for the hosted_vllm provider during Router fallback handling.

The stack shows the `/responses` proxy path calling into Router, then into `litellm.responses` / `litellm.aresponses`, which in turn call `litellm.acompletion` / `litellm.completion`. Inside completion, `get_optional_params()` invokes `add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params()`, which assumes `extra_body` is always a mapping and attempts to unpack it via `**extra_body`. When `extra_body` happens to be `None`, this raises the TypeError.
```


## What did you expect to happen?

- `/responses` requests should:
  - Either succeed and return a valid OpenAI Responses API payload, or
  - Fail with a cleanly surfaced upstream provider error (e.g. hosted_vllm connection/fallback error),
- **But not** raise an internal `TypeError` due to `extra_body` being `None`.

In particular:
- Passing `extra_body=None` (explicitly or indirectly through other flows) should be treated equivalently to not passing `extra_body` at all — that is, as if `extra_body={}`.
- Router fallback behavior should not be impacted by whether `extra_body` is `None`.

---

## Root Cause Analysis (Code-Level)

The error is triggered in two related places when handling OpenAI-compatible providers:

1. In the OpenAI-like chat handler’s `completion()` method:
   - File: `litellm/llms/openai_like/chat/handler.py`
   - Function: `OpenAILikeChatHandler.completion()`

   Relevant code (before the fix):

   ```python
   stream: bool = optional_params.pop("stream", None) or False
   extra_body = optional_params.pop("extra_body", {})
   json_mode = optional_params.pop("json_mode", None)
   optional_params.pop("max_retries", None)
   ...
   data = {
       "model": model,
       "messages": messages,
       **optional_params,
       **extra_body,
   }
   ```

   If `extra_body` is `None` (e.g., because an upstream caller explicitly set `extra_body=None`), `**extra_body` raises `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not a mapping`.

2. In `add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params()` used by `get_optional_params()` for OpenAI-compatible providers and `litellm.openai_compatible_providers` (including `hosted_vllm`):
   - File: `litellm/utils.py`
   - Function: `add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params()`

   Relevant code (before the fix):

   ```python
   if (
       custom_llm_provider in ["openai", "azure", "text-completion-openai"]
       + litellm.openai_compatible_providers
   ):
       if (
           _should_drop_param(
               k="extra_body", additional_drop_params=additional_drop_params
           )
           is False
       ):
           extra_body = passed_params.pop("extra_body", {})
           for k in passed_params.keys():
               if k not in openai_params and passed_params[k] is not None:
                   extra_body[k] = passed_params[k]
           optional_params.setdefault("extra_body", {})
           initial_extra_body = {
               **optional_params["extra_body"],
               **extra_body,
           }
           ...
   ```

   Here, `passed_params.pop("extra_body", {})` (or `optional_params["extra_body"]`) can also be `None`, and again unpacking it with `**extra_body` or `**optional_params["extra_body"]` raises the same TypeError.

In both locations, the code assumes that `extra_body` and `optional_params["extra_body"]` are always mappings (`dict`), but in practice they can be `None`.

---

## Proposed Fix (Patch)

The fix is to normalize any `None` value of `extra_body` (and `optional_params["extra_body"]`) to `{}` before unpacking.

### 1) Normalize `extra_body` in `OpenAILikeChatHandler.completion()`

File: `litellm/llms/openai_like/chat/handler.py`

```diff
diff --git a/litellm/llms/openai_like/chat/handler.py b/litellm/llms/openai_like/chat/handler.py
index 821fc9b7f1..3f8e535bef 100644
--- a/litellm/llms/openai_like/chat/handler.py
+++ b/litellm/llms/openai_like/chat/handler.py
@@ -254,7 +254,7 @@ class OpenAILikeChatHandler(OpenAILikeBase):
         )
 
         stream: bool = optional_params.pop("stream", None) or False
-        extra_body = optional_params.pop("extra_body", {})
+        extra_body = optional_params.pop("extra_body", {}) or {}
         json_mode = optional_params.pop("json_mode", None)
         optional_params.pop("max_retries", None)
         if not fake_stream:
```

**Rationale:**
- If `extra_body` is `None`, `optional_params.pop("extra_body", {}) or {}` will normalize it to `{}`.
- The subsequent `data = {..., **optional_params, **extra_body}` remains safe.
- When `extra_body` is already a dict, behavior is unchanged.

### 2) Normalize `extra_body` and `optional_params["extra_body"]` in `add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params()`

File: `litellm/utils.py`

```diff
diff --git a/litellm/utils.py b/litellm/utils.py
index 4771219d11..9c6357dbbd 100644
--- a/litellm/utils.py
+++ b/litellm/utils.py
@@ -4644,13 +4644,15 @@ def add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params(
             )
             is False
         ):
-            extra_body = passed_params.pop("extra_body", {})
+            extra_body = passed_params.pop("extra_body", {}) or {}
             for k in passed_params.keys():
                 if k not in openai_params and passed_params[k] is not None:
                     extra_body[k] = passed_params[k]
             optional_params.setdefault("extra_body", {})
+            # Handle case where extra_body is already set to None
+            _extra_body_from_optional = optional_params.get("extra_body") or {}
             initial_extra_body = {
-                **optional_params["extra_body"],
+                **_extra_body_from_optional,
                 **extra_body,
             }
```

**Rationale:**
- `passed_params.pop("extra_body", {}) or {}` ensures `extra_body` is always a dict.
- `optional_params.get("extra_body") or {}` ensures that even if `optional_params["extra_body"]` exists but is `None`, it is treated as an empty dict for merging.
- When both `extra_body` and `optional_params["extra_body"]` are dicts, behavior is unchanged: they are merged with later values taking precedence.

---

## Impact and Backwards Compatibility

- The change is minimal and localized to two places where `extra_body` is unpacked.
- For existing code where `extra_body` and `optional_params["extra_body"]` are always dicts, behavior is unchanged.
- For cases where `extra_body` is `None` (either explicitly or via upstream transformations), the new behavior is to treat it as `{}`, which matches typical expectations for optional mapping-type parameters.
- This fix prevents the internal `TypeError` and allows Router + `/responses` flows with OpenAI-compatible providers to either succeed or surface real provider errors without crashing.


### Steps to Reproduce

This minimal reproduction does not require hosted_vllm model or `/responses`; it should use the same underlying code path in the core library.

```python
import litellm

litellm._turn_on_debug()

# Configure OPENAI_API_KEY via environment or litellm config as usual.

response = litellm.responses(
    model="openai/gpt-4o",  # any OpenAI-compatible provider should hit this path
    input="hi",
    extra_body=None,         # key detail: explicitly passing None
)
```

**Actual behavior:**
- Raises `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not a mapping` from within the OpenAI-compatible parameter handling.

**Expected behavior:**
- The call should behave as if `extra_body={}` (no additional fields) and either:
  - Successfully call OpenAI and return a response, or
  - Raise a provider-specific error if something else is wrong, but **not** a TypeError.

I believe the same applies to `litellm.aresponses`, `litellm.completion`, and `litellm.acompletion` when used with OpenAI-compatible providers.

---

## Real-World Reproduction (My setup: LiteLLM Proxy + simple_shuffle router + hosted_vllm glm-4.7-flash)

1. Configure LiteLLM proxy with a Router model group pointing to a hosted_vllm deployment for `glm-4.7-flash`, plus model group fallbacks if you want (e.g. to other providers/models).
2. Start the proxy Docker container.
3. From an OpenAI-compatible client (kilocode in my case), configure LiteLLM proxy as the provider and call:
   - `POST /responses` with `model: "glm-4.7-flash"` and an input.
4. Observe that every request returns HTTP 500.

**Actual result:**
- Client receives a 500 error with a message containing:

  ```text
  Hosted_vllmException - 'NoneType' object is not a mapping
  No fallback model group found for original model_group=glm-4.7-flash.
  ```

- Proxy logs show an internal `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not a mapping` inside the OpenAI-compatible parameter handling (`add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params` and the OpenAI-like chat handler).

**Expected result:**
- Successful response from hosted_vllm or
- Cleanly surfaced hosted_vllm / Router error without an internal TypeError.

---


### Relevant log output

```shell
### Docker logs

litellm.proxy.proxy_server._handle_llm_api_exception(): Exception occured - litellm.APIConnectionError: APIConnectionError: Hosted_vllmException - 'NoneType' object is not a mapping
No fallback model group found for original model_group=glm-4.7-flash-reap.
Fallbacks=[{"google/gemini-2.5-pro": ["google/gemini-2.5-flash"]}, {"Llama-3.1-70b": ["Gemma3-27b"]}, ...]
Received Model Group=glm-4.7-flash-reap
Available Model Group Fallbacks=None
Error doing the fallback: litellm.APIConnectionError: APIConnectionError: Hosted_vllmException - 'NoneType' object is not a mapping
...

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/litellm/main.py", line 1457, in completion
    optional_params = get_optional_params(...)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/litellm/utils.py", line 4599, in get_optional_params
    optional_params = add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params(...)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/litellm/utils.py", line 4652, in add_provider_specific_params_to_optional_params
    initial_extra_body = {
        **optional_params["extra_body"],
        **extra_body,
    }
TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not a mapping

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

  File "/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/litellm/proxy/response_api_endpoints/endpoints.py", line 160, in responses_api
    response = await processor.base_process_llm_request(...)
  ...
  File "/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/litellm/responses/litellm_completion_transformation/handler.py", line 118, in async_response_api_handler

  File "/usr/lib/python3.13/site-packages/litellm/main.py", line 4305, in completion

BerriAI/litellm#22215.body:
## Summary
- **Bug**: Models added via the UI are saved to the database but may fail to sync into the router (due to decryption errors, invalid params with `ignore_invalid_deployments=True`, etc.). When this happens, the models become invisible in the Model Management page because `/v2/model/info` only reads from `llm_router.model_list`.
- **Root cause**: `_apply_search_filter_to_models()` returned early with only router models when no search term was provided (line 8882). The DB fallback query only ran during search.
- **Fix**: Extract DB-query logic into `_get_db_only_models()` and call it in all cases (search and non-search). This ensures models stored in the DB but not loaded in the router still appear in the UI.
- When there are no DB-only models and no search, the original behavior is preserved (returns `None` for total_count).

## Customer Impact
A customer could register models via the Ui, but even having the models confirmed in the database, it is possible for them to never appear in the model list for the Ui. The backend `/models` and `/v2/model/info` endpoints could not return them, given the way the internal router retrieves/builds the model list.

## Test plan
- [x] 8 new unit tests covering all paths (`tests/test_litellm/proxy/test_model_list_db_only_models.py`)
  - DB-only models returned when present
  - No DB-only models preserves original behavior
  - Search filtering works with DB-only models
  - Config models not incorrectly excluded
  - No prisma_client graceful fallback

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
BerriAI/litellm#22215.title: fix(proxy): show DB models in model list even when router sync fails

BerriAI/litellm#22240.body:
### Check for existing issues

- [x] I have searched the existing issues and checked that my issue is not a duplicate.

### What happened?

When replaying multi-turn conversations that include Anthropic server tool blocks (`server_tool_use` + `*_tool_result`), LiteLLM keeps the `server_tool_use` block but drops the paired `*_tool_result` block. Anthropic then returns 400 because every tool-use block must have a corresponding result.

This affects any Anthropic server tool (code execution, web search, etc.) when prior turns are replayed.

## Repro

Two scripts are attached:

- **`repro_litellm_server_tool_history.py`** — sends a multi-turn conversation through LiteLLM with server tool blocks in history. **Fails** because the tool result is dropped.
- **`repro_anthropic_server_tool_history.py`** — same messages sent directly via the Anthropic SDK. **Passes.**

```bash
pip install litellm anthropic
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=...
python repro_litellm_server_tool_history.py    # fails
python repro_anthropic_server_tool_history.py  # passes (control)
```

The LiteLLM script also dumps the outgoing request from debug logs so you can confirm the tool result block is missing.

## Root cause (two related issues)

### 1. Raw block passthrough drops `*_tool_result` blocks

When assistant content is a list of blocks, `anthropic_messages_pt` whitelists `server_tool_use` but does not pass through `*_tool_result` blocks (e.g. `bash_code_execution_tool_result`).

Likely location: `litellm/litellm_core_utils/prompt_templates/factory.py` around lines 2471–2478.

Also in the request transformation path: `litellm/llms/anthropic/chat/transformation.py` around lines 1335–1339.

### 2. `provider_specific_fields` round-trip is fundamentally wrong for these blocks

Server tool results are **content blocks** — they live in the assistant message's `content` array alongside `text` and `tool_use` blocks. But LiteLLM pulls them out of `content` and stashes them in `provider_specific_fields["tool_results"]`, a metadata side-channel. This means they're no longer part of the message and require special reconstruction to round-trip.

That reconstruction is also incomplete: it only reads `provider_specific_fields["web_search_results"]`, so non-web-search tool results are silently lost even when using LiteLLM's own response objects.

The fix should keep these blocks in the message content (like the raw block passthrough in issue 1), not move them to a side-channel that requires manual reconstruction.

Potential pointers:
- Response parsing extracts blocks into `provider_specific_fields["tool_results"]`: `litellm/llms/anthropic/chat/transformation.py` ~lines 1705–1706
- Request reconstruction reads only `["web_search_results"]`: `litellm/litellm_core_utils/prompt_templates/factory.py` ~lines 2507–2523
- `convert_to_anthropic_tool_invoke` appends only `web_search_tool_result`: `litellm/litellm_core_utils/prompt_templates/factory.py` ~lines 1843–1848

## Expected behavior

LiteLLM should preserve all `*_tool_result` blocks paired with `server_tool_use` blocks when forwarding assistant history to Anthropic — not just `web_search_tool_result`.

## Assistant history shape for reference

This is what a valid assistant turn looks like with server tool blocks. The `server_tool_use` and `bash_code_execution_tool_result` must both be present:

```json
{
  "role": "assistant",
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "server_tool_use",
      "id": "srvtoolu_123",
      "name": "bash_code_execution",
      "input": { "command": "python3 -c \"print(2)\"" },
      "caller": { "type": "direct" }
    },
    {
      "type": "bash_code_execution_tool_result",
      "tool_use_id": "srvtoolu_123",
      "content": {
        "type": "bash_code_execution_result",
        "stdout": "2\n",
        "stderr": "",
        "return_code": 0,
        "content": []
      }
    },
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "The answer is 2."
    }
  ]
}
```

## References

- Anthropic: [Server tool use](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use/implement-tool-use)
- Anthropic: [Code execution](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/code-execution)
- PydanticAI server tool round-tripping — parse: [`anthropic.py#L555`](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/blob/v1.63.0/pydantic_ai_slim/pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py#L555), replay: [`anthropic.py#L880`](https://github.com/pydantic/pydantic-ai/blob/v1.63.0/pydantic_ai_slim/pydantic_ai/models/anthropic.py#L880)


### Steps to Reproduce

[repro_litellm_server_tool_history.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25589484/repro_litellm_server_tool_history.py)
[repro_anthropic_server_tool_history.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25589485/repro_anthropic_server_tool_history.py)

### Relevant log output

```shell

```

### What part of LiteLLM is this about?

SDK (litellm Python package)

### What LiteLLM version are you on ?

1.81.10

_No response_

BerriAI/litellm#22330.body:
## Bug Description

All 4 MCP e2e tests in `tests/mcp_tests/test_proxy_mcp_e2e.py` fail on main with auth errors:

```
AssertionError: assert 'Error: User not allowed to call this tool.' == '30'
```

### Failing Tests
- `TestProxyMcpSimpleConnections::test_proxy_mcp_stdio_roundtrip`
- `TestProxyMcpSimpleConnections::test_proxy_mcp_streamable_http_roundtrip`
- `TestProxyMcpSimpleConnections::test_proxy_mcp_lists_all_servers_without_header`
- `TestProxyMcpStatelessBehavior::test_independent_clients_no_shared_session`

### Root Cause

`set_auth_context()` at `server.py:2063` stores auth info (user_api_key_auth, mcp_servers, etc.) in a `ContextVar` during the HTTP request handler. However, the MCP `StreamableHTTPSessionManager` spawns new `anyio.TaskGroup` tasks for handling protocol messages like `call_tool` and `list_tools`.

`ContextVar` values don't reliably propagate into these spawned tasks, so when `get_auth_context()` is called inside `mcp_server_tool_call` (line 302), it returns empty/stale data. This causes `get_allowed_mcp_servers()` to return an empty list → `_get_allowed_mcp_servers_from_mcp_server_names` returns empty → 403 "User not allowed to call this tool."

### Relevant Code

- `set_auth_context()` — `server.py:2197`
- `get_auth_context()` — `server.py:2227`
- `mcp_server_tool_call` reads context — `server.py:302`
- `handle_streamable_http_mcp` sets context — `server.py:2063`

### Suggested Fixes

1. **Store auth on the MCP session object** — persist auth across protocol messages within a session
2. **Pass auth explicitly** through the MCP handler chain instead of relying on ContextVars
3. **Use `copy_context().run()`** when spawning tasks in the session manager to inherit parent context

### Environment
- All tests fail locally on main (macOS, Python 3.12+)
- Also fails in GitHub Actions CI

### Related
- Discovered while investigating CI failures on PR #22327
- Regression test for https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/issues/20242 (stateless mode)
BerriAI/litellm#22330.title: bug: MCP e2e tests fail — auth ContextVar not propagated into SessionManager task groups

BerriAI/litellm#23472.body:
## Relevant issues

Fixes Prisma connection loss after ~60-70 minutes on installs with large numbers of stale managed objects (e.g. 336K queued response rows).

## Pre-Submission checklist

- [ ] I have Added testing in the [`tests/test_litellm/`](https://github.com/BerriAI/litellm/tree/main/tests/test_litellm) directory, **Adding at least 1 test is a hard requirement** - [see details](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/extras/contributing_code)
- [ ] My PR passes all unit tests on [`make test-unit`](https://docs.litellm.ai/docs/extras/contributing_code)
- [ ] My PR's scope is as isolated as possible, it only solves 1 specific problem
- [ ] I have requested a Greptile review by commenting `@greptileai` and received a **Confidence Score of at least 4/5** before requesting a maintainer review

## CI (LiteLLM team)

- [ ] **Branch creation CI run**  
       Link:

- [ ] **CI run for the last commit**  
       Link:

- [ ] **Merge / cherry-pick CI run**  
       Links:

## Type

🐛 Bug Fix

## Changes

Root cause: `CheckResponsesCost` and `CheckBatchCost` poll `LiteLLM_ManagedObjectTable` with no row limit. One affected customer had 336K stale `queued` response rows — loading all of them OOM-killed the Prisma Rust binary, breaking the localhost HTTP connection permanently.

Three fixes:

**1. Stale-row cleanup** — at the start of each poll cycle, rows older than `MANAGED_OBJECT_STALENESS_CUTOFF_DAYS` (default 7 days) in non-terminal states are bulk-updated to `stale_expired`. They'll never complete and shouldn't be polled.

**2. Pagination** — both `find_many` queries now use `take=MAX_OBJECTS_PER_POLL_CYCLE` (default 50) with `order={"created_at": "asc"}`. Unbounded fetches are gone.

**3. `batch_processed` column fallback** — `CheckBatchCost` wraps its query in a try/except; if the `batch_processed` column doesn't exist (older schema without the `20260222000000` migration), it falls back to a query without that filter instead of silently erroring every cycle.

**4. Kill-switch env flag** — `PROXY_BATCH_POLLING_ENABLED=false` skips scheduling both jobs entirely. Useful for emergency mitigation while cleaning up stale rows manually.

New constants (all env-configurable):
- `PROXY_BATCH_POLLING_ENABLED` (default `true`)
- `MAX_OBJECTS_PER_POLL_CYCLE` (default `50`)
- `MANAGED_OBJECT_STALENESS_CUTOFF_DAYS` (default `7`)
